title: Cleaner Earns More Than Me: Pharmacist's Viral Video Sparks Healthcare Salary Debate
summary: New Delhi: A viral Instagram video by a pharmacist has sparked a widespread debate on the salaries offered to qualified healthcare professionals after she claimed she was offered a monthly salary of...
thbAction: Map the news to THB's provider CRM for referral growth, patient lifecycle engagement, and multi-facility relationship management across hospital and provider networks.
contentHtml:
New Delhi: A viral Instagram video by a pharmacist has sparked a widespread debate on the salaries offered to qualified healthcare professionals after she claimed she was offered a monthly salary of Rs 12,000-14,000 for a pharmacist's position at a hospital where, according to her, a cleaning staff member was earning Rs 16,000 per month.

The video has garnered nearly nine lakh views, prompting discussions on salary disparities, entry-level pay and the value placed on healthcare professionals.

In the video, the pharmacist, identified as Asha, recounted visiting a hospital to inquire about a pharmacist vacancy. Before attending the interview, she said she spoke with a woman mopping the hospital floor and asked about her monthly salary. According to Asha, the cleaner replied that she earned ₹16,000 a month. Based on that conversation, Asha said she expected a pharmacist's salary to be at least ₹18,000–20,000, considering the qualifications and responsibilities associated with the role.

During the interview, the hospital's HR representative reportedly asked whether she had prior experience working as a pharmacist. Asha responded that while she was a fresher in pharmacy, she had four years of experience in the healthcare sector. She alleged that the HR informed her that fresh pharmacy graduates were offered a basic salary ranging from ₹12,000 to ₹14,000, with the exact amount depending on performance. According to her, the HR also stated that the hospital generally did not recruit freshers, but when it did, the starting salary remained within that range. Asha said she was shocked by the offer, laughed, and walked out of the interview.

Also Read: BFUHS Pharmacist Recruitment 2026: Admit Cards Out for 454 Pharmacy Officer Posts

The video triggered mixed reactions online. While several users questioned the low salary offered to a qualified pharmacist, others argued that comparing the salary of a cleaner with that of a pharmacist did not present the complete picture, pointing out that the cleaner may have spent years in the job to reach the reported salary level. Responding to such comments, Asha clarified that her intention was not to compare professions or undermine the work of housekeeping staff but to draw attention to the low starting salaries offered to qualified healthcare professionals.

The discussion soon expanded beyond the viral video, with many healthcare professionals sharing similar experiences. One user commented that their first salary as a doctor was ₹7,500, while another said dentists often begin their careers with salaries of around ₹10,000. Others claimed that several hospitals expect professionals to work 10 to 12 hours a day while offering salaries between ₹12,000 and ₹15,000, with deductions further reducing their take-home pay.

According to The Times of India, the viral video has reignited discussions over salary structures for healthcare professionals, particularly fresh graduates entering the workforce. The publication noted that the claims made in the video, including the salary figures and interview details, have not been independently verified.

M. Pharm (Pharmaceutics)

Parthika Patel has completed her Graduated B.Pharm from SSR COLLEGE OF PHARMACY and done M.Pharm in Pharmaceutics. She can be contacted at editorial@medicaldialogues.in. Contact no. 011-43720751
